journal: Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences of the United States of America
title: Modular genetic architecture underlies human hand and foot evolution
abstract: The functional divergence of the hand and foot for prehension and locomotion, respectively, has long been recognized as a critical event in human evolution, and involved substantial changes to the skeleton. Hominins evolved a more muscular and opposable thumb while the other fingers are relatively shorter, enhancing manipulative capacity. The feet evolved robust first toes and short lateral toes to meet the challenges of bipedal walking and running. While adaptations in the hand and foot have often been considered separately, the fore- and hind limbs of primates are morphologically integrated, serially homologous structures, raising the possibility that natural selection on either autopod may have driven corresponding changes in the other. To explore the genetic architecture underlying human autopod evolution, we used functional genomics methods to identify regulatory elements and gene expression in the developing phalanges and metacarpals of the human hand and foot. We identified genetic modules based on patterns of gene expression or regulatory activity, which strongly distinguish the metapodials from the phalanges and separate tissues to a lesser extent along the anterior–posterior axis or between limb types. We show that some of these genetic modules are enriched for human-specific genomic features, which potentially underlie anatomical differences in the autopod skeleton between humans and other apes. We find stronger enrichments for human-specific genomic features related to the foot than the hand, consistent with models hypothesizing stronger selective pressure on the human foot during the transition to bipedalism. Our results highlight the modular genetic architecture underlying human autopod evolution.
discussion: DiscussionThe functional divergence of the upper and lower limbs is a critical event in hominin evolution and involved substantial remodeling of the postcranial skeleton. To further our understanding of the genetic mechanisms underlying these skeletal changes, we used functional genomics methods to investigate gene expression and regulation during development of the distal autopod skeleton in humans. We identified thousands of regulatory elements and genes involved in the developing human hand and foot skeleton and classified them into genetic modules based on shared patterns of gene expression or accessibility, respectively. These data reveal substantial differences in gene expression and regulation between the phalanges and metapodials and between the two timepoints, with more limited differences between digits or limb types. Consistent with our first prediction, we found that regulatory and gene expression modules have similar profiles in the hand and foot as evidenced by the strong correlation between module eigengene values for homologous tissues of the hand and feet, providing a genetic basis for models of human limb evolution based on strong morphological integration (i.e., elevated phenotypic covariance) between homologous elements of the fore- and hind limbs .Of the modules we identified, we found that some are enriched for human-specific genomic features and could potentially underlie the evolution of human-specific autopod traits (Fig. 5). hCONDELs (at the early stage) and inversions (at both stages) were enriched in regulatory elements patterning the phalanges and may have contributed to the reduction in phalangeal length of the posterior digits in humans compared to chimps (Fig. 1B). For differences between limb types related to the evolution of bipedalism, we identified only three modules with a strong bias toward either the hand (Late Module 1; Fig. 4A1) or foot (Early Module 4, and Late Module 4; Figs. 3A4 and 4A4). The hand-biased Late Module 1 was enriched for hCONDELs and inversions, implicating these genomic features in autopod evolution. The foot-biased Early Module 4, which has accessibility only in Metatarsals of digits II-V, did not have any significant enrichments. The story of foot-specific evolutionary signals is more complicated for the second foot-biased module due to the extent of shared peaks between that module (Late Module 4) and Early Module 3 which has accessibility in both the hand and foot (Fig. 3A3). Late Module 4 has many more regulatory elements (1,406 vs. 618), however, suggesting that many of the peaks do not share the early signal. Late Module 4 is enriched for HAQERs, SDRs, and SDR flanking regions. Early Module 3 shows weaker enrichments for all these terms except for SDRs, which was not identified as significant at all. Overall, the enrichments for SDRs and the flanking regions suggest that genomic structural rearrangements may have played a role in rewiring regulatory elements related to foot development, potentially leading to phenotypic changes. Future work is necessary to understand the precise regulatory consequences of these SDRs. Overall, we find more signals of human-specific sequence evolution–with greater statistical support (lower adjusted P-values and higher odds ratio at both stages)–for the foot-specific module compared to the hand-specific module (Fig. 5). This provides partial support for our second prediction that selection has been greater on the foot than the hand .Linking the identified genomic changes to observed evolutionary changes in anatomy remains a major challenge, especially on rare human materials . While our data reveal genetic modules that may have been the targets of natural selection to drive adaptations in the human autopod, we cannot rule out the possibility that a few mutations in elements either within or outside these modules had large contributions to human evolution. This is a limitation of our focus on modules which emphasizes patterns consistent across multiple genes or regulatory elements. Nonetheless, the modules identified here have the potential to explain the differences in length of the posterior phalanges and overall changes in hand and foot, separately. Notably absent in our data, however, is a genetic module(s) defining digit I, which has substantial anatomical change between humans and chimps. Instead, we find modules that characterize the posterior digits to the exclusion of digit I. This is in contrast to our findings in mice (SI Appendix, Fig. S3), where multiple modules contained just digit I elements. In humans, DEGs and DARs associated with differences between digit I and digit II were spread across multiple modules. This suggests the possibility that the greater size and width observed in both the phalanges and metapodials of the hallux and pollex are due to genetic changes with pleiotropic impacts on other autopod tissues. The relevant changes may also occur at a different stage of development than those investigated in this study. For example, the relatively robust size of the first digits already observable at E54 suggests that regulatory elements specific to digit I may be accessible earlier in development (Fig. 1D).To fully understand the genetic underpinning of human autopod evolution, future studies are necessary to explore additional timepoints and to generate comparable data on the bones of the wrist, midfoot, and hindfoot. These proximal autopod bones have also undergone substantial evolution in the human lineage and are undoubtedly directly relevant to the evolution of the metapodials and structure of the autopod in general. Exploration of individual phalanges might reveal additional modules with evolutionary relevance, as each phalangeal region forms a relatively independent anatomical module , Similarly, future studies should investigate the genomic changes underlying evolution in the soft tissues of the autopod. As this study was based on bulk chondrocyte tissue dissections, future research would also benefit from techniques with greater resolution, such as spatial multiomics, to unravel the genetic basis of these human-specific features with greater anatomical precision . Such methods could investigate human-specific changes in shape related to load bearing and the articulation of bones with one another. Furthermore, these methods could explore the impacts of extrinsic signaling and mechanical impacts from neighboring tissues, such as mesenchyme and muscle, on autopod skeletal development . Given the intricate functional, developmental, and evolutionary relationships between all parts of the autopod, a holistic approach that integrates datasets from multiple tissue types will ultimately be necessary to understand the evolution of human-specific features. Nevertheless, this study provides insights into the evolutionary history of human bipedalism by highlighting the modular genetic architecture underlying human autopod evolution.
figure_legends: Anatomy of the human and chimpanzee autopod skeleton. (A), morphology of the adult hand (Top) and foot (Bottom) skeleton in humans (Left) and chimpanzees (Right). (B), average percent change in length and (C), width between adult human and chimpanzee autopod skeletal elements. (D), Alizarin red/Alcian blue bone/cartilage staining of developmental human autopod skeletons at gestational day E54 and E67. The intermediate and distal phalanges of digit III in the E50s sample were lost during sample preparation, indicated by an *. Metatarsal I in the E60 to 70s sample is broken and has been digitally corrected. Note that the phalanges are splayed due to the removal of soft tissue. Photos courtesy of M. Young.

Gene expression and regulation in the human autopod. PC analysis of (A) normalized human gene count data, or (B) normalized accessibility data, corrected for biological replicate. Volcano plot showing the differences in (C) gene expression or (D) accessibility between early and late samples. In (C) the Top 10 most significant DEGs in each direction between the early and late stages are labeled. In (D) the Top 10 most significant DARs in each direction between the early and late stages are labeled with their nearest gene. Timepoint differences are not seen in (A and B) due to correction for biological replicate in the DESeq2 model.

Human early gene regulation and expression modules. Average module eigengene expression for (A) DAR and (B) DEG modules identified by WGCNA of early-stage samples, plotted onto a diagram of the autopod skeleton to show the spatial arrangement. Modules are arranged in descending order of the number of DARs or DEGs within the module and numbered accordingly in the figure (A1-7, B1-4). Red reflects a relative increase and blue a relative decrease in accessibility/expression in the colored bone primordia. In (A and B) a brief summary of the most enriched GO terms is provided for each module; complete enrichments are available in Datasets S2 and S3. dev. = development; resp. = response; abnorm. = abnormal; org. = organization; ECM = extracellular matrix; diff. = differentiation. (C) GREAT enrichments of DEG modules for each DAR module. Only significant enrichments (Padj < 0.05) are shown. For visualization, all Padj < 10−10 were set equal to 10−10. (D) heatmap showing the percent of transcription factors (TFs) in each DEG module with an enriched motif in any DAR module that are enriched in each DAR module.

Human late gene regulation and expression modules. Average module eigengene expression for (A) DAR and (B) DEG modules identified by WGCNA of late-stage samples, plotted onto a diagram of the autopod skeleton to show the spatial arrangement. See Fig. 3 caption for details. organ. fiss. = organelle fission; homol. rec. = homologous recombination; endo. = endothelium; cytop. trans. = cytoplasmic translation; prox-dist. patt. form. = proximal/distal pattern formation. (C) GREAT enrichments of DEG modules for each DAR module. Only significant enrichments (Padj < 0.05) are shown. For visualization, all Padj < 10−10 were set equal to 10−10. (D) heatmap showing the percent of TFs in each DEG module with an enriched motif in any DAR module that are enriched in each DAR module.

Enrichment of evolutionary signals in autopod regulatory modules. GREAT enrichments of common variants (MAF > 0.05) in humans, chimpanzees, and gorillas, autopod regulatory elements with conserved accessibility in mice, HARs, HAQERs, hCONDELs, human-specific inversions, human SDRs, and their flanking sequences and regulatory elements identified as differentially active via a massively parallel reporter assay for all DARs as well as each DAR module. Only DAR modules with significant enrichments are shown but all overlap sets are shown even if there were no significant enrichments for any DAR set.
